title: Thermal Ecology in R
text:
Welcome
This book is a practical guide for anyone working with thermal ecological data using R. It focuses specifically on the processes and challenges you’ll face with such data, and provides best practices for reporting.
The intended audience is for graduate students new to thermal ecology and experts in the field who are unfamiliar with R. This book will mainly follow the tidyverse syntax for consistency in learning experience.
If you are new with R and not use basis data manipulation and coding, I have a series of tutorials for undergraduate students working with ecological data. Most relevant for this audience is week 1–4 contents (quality check, tidy data, diversity indices, and descriptive stats).
The tutorials teach you how to check for the quality of the raw data you imported, the steps to correct errors in the raw data, how transform raw data to something usable for analysis, and get familiar with clean code.

Learn how to load and clean raw data from own data loggers, access climate databases and microclimate simulations, and calculating metrics of heatwaves.

title: Respirometry
text:
Aquatic Respirometry
Authors: Nicholas Wu
Estimating metabolic rate
We will use the respR package to illustrate how to process and tidy raw oxygen traces from a respirometry experiment. The package has also been peer reviewed and published in Methods in Ecology and Evolution.
The tutorial will use data from a close-chamber respiromety experiment, but the package is able to handle intermittent-flow and open-flow respirometry, and critical oxygen values (Pcrit).

title: Respirometry
text:
Setting up
Load R packages and set up directory and load your raw data. At minimum, the raw data should contain a time column and the oxygen column. In this example, we have xxxx.

#install.packages("respR")

# Load libraries
library(respR)
library(tidyverse)

# load data
